title: Triple chocolate layer cake
subtitle: Three cocoa sponge layers, each with a different chocolate filling and a dark ganache finish.
excerpt: Triple chocolate layer cake stacks three thin cocoa sponge layers with white chocolate cream, milk chocolate buttercream, and a dark chocolate ganache poured over the top. Each layer is made with the same sponge, so the baking is straightforward; the complexity is in the three different fillings. This is a celebration cake.
sourceNotes: Multi-layer chocolate cakes became popular in American baking in the late nineteenth century, partly influenced by the availability of commercial cocoa powder after 1866 (Van Houten's Dutch-process method) and the spread of baking powder. The American layer cake format reached Britain through food media from the 1980s onwards. Triple chocolate cakes using all three main chocolate types appear in professional patisserie from the 1990s. This recipe synthesises the American layered-cake structure with European ganache finishing, following standard British baking proportions for the sponge.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted sponge layers wrapped separately. Defrost at room temperature before assembling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge layers can be baked two days ahead and stored wrapped at room temperature. The assembled cake keeps in the fridge for three days; remove 30 minutes before serving.

recipeTools.notes: Three 20 cm round tins; or bake in batches using fewer tins.
For smoothing the layers and sides.
Heatproof bowl for melting chocolate over simmering water.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of hot cream and chocolate, stirred until smooth. At room temperature it is pourable and glossy; when chilled it firms up to a spreadable consistency. The ratio of cream to chocolate determines the final texture: more cream gives a flowing ganache; less gives a firmer truffle-like result.

content.text: Triple chocolate cake is three layers of the same cocoa sponge with three different chocolate fillings: white chocolate cream between the first two layers, milk chocolate buttercream between the second and third, and a dark chocolate
poured over the whole thing. Each element is straightforward on its own. The challenge is coordinating three fillings without losing your nerve.

Make the sponge layers first and let them cool completely. Then make the fillings in order: white chocolate cream, milk chocolate buttercream, dark ganache. Work from top to bottom and keep each layer in the fridge as you go.
Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line three 20 cm round tins (or bake in batches).
Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Beat in {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, and {{whole-milk}} until combined.
Divide the batter equally between three tins. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes each. Cool completely on racks before filling.

White chocolate cream: heat {{double-cream}} to a simmer, pour over {{white-chocolate}}, stir until melted. Cool to room temperature, then chill for 30 minutes until the consistency of thick cream. Whisk briefly before using.
Milk chocolate buttercream: beat {{unsalted-butter}} until pale and creamy. Add {{icing-sugar}} in two additions. Beat in {{milk-chocolate}} until smooth. Continue beating until light and fluffy.
Dark ganache: heat {{double-cream}} to a simmer, pour over {{dark-chocolate}}, stir until completely smooth. Leave to cool to a pourable but not hot consistency, about 20 to 30 minutes.

Place the first sponge layer on a board. Spread with the white chocolate cream. Add the second layer and spread with the milk chocolate buttercream. Add the third layer. Smooth any buttercream around the sides to seal the edges.
Pour the dark ganache over the top of the assembled cake, letting it drip down the sides. Leave at room temperature for at least 20 minutes for the ganache to set before serving.
